sessions.title: The Geopolitics of Fossil Fuels. Seminar by the Istituto Affari Internazionali (Rome)
topics: The energy trilemma: balancing security, equity, and sustainability
1973 oil embargo: three simultaneous crises (geopolitical/Yom Kippur, energy market, US political/Watergate); ended March 18, 1974
US shale revolution: shale gas ~70% and tight oil ~60% of US production by Dec 2018; US becomes world's top producer
Geopolitical effects of shale: pressure on Russia (gas) and Saudi Arabia (oil); enabled US Pivot to Asia
EU-Russia gas dependence: 155 bcm (~40% of EU supply) in 2021; collapse of Wandel durch Handel paradigm post-2022
Post-2022 EU diversification: Norway 30% of EU pipeline imports, US 50% of EU LNG imports
2026 Strait of Hormuz Crisis: 20 mb/d oil (25% global seaborne), 110 bcm LNG (20% global LNG)
Petrostates vs Electrostates: contrasting geopolitical actor types in fossil fuel vs energy transition eras

sessions.title: Geopolitics and Connectivity: the Belt and Road Initiative

sessions.title: Guest Lecture: Against Geopolitics. Andrea Ruggeri, Università di Milano
topics: Critical perspectives on geopolitical thinking and its limits

What geopolitical frameworks omit: norms, institutions, agency, and ideas

sessions.title: Summing Up: From Globalization to Fragmentation. Cui Prodest?
topics: A world in transition across five dimensions: political systems, international relations, culture, technology, and economy
Historical cycles of globalization: first wave (1815-1914), deglobalization (1920s-1950s), second wave (1950s-2010s), slowbalization (2010s-)
Effects of globalization: convergence and equality between countries, but growing inequality within countries
Populism on the rise: domestic losers of globalization driving protectionism
Globalization beneficial for revisionist autocracies, contributing to rising multipolarity and retreat of internationalism

The 'crashing apps': technology (common good → global threat), institutions (crisis of Washington Consensus), culture (cosmopolitan → localism), IR (return of zero-sum realism), geopolitics (hegemony → multipolar assertiveness), political systems (populism and autocracy rising)
Multiple futures: Slowbalization (suboptimal but stable), Two Worlds Apart (bloc division, worst for developing countries), Armageddon (already happened historically), Rise of Stakeholder Geopolitics

definitions.definition: Study of how geography, resources, and spatial distribution of power influence international relations and state behaviour
International system where two superpowers dominate; characteristic of Cold War era (US vs USSR)
International system with multiple centres of power; several major powers balance and compete
International system dominated by a single superpower; characteristic of 1990s-2000s under US hegemony
Use of economic tools and interdependencies as instruments of geopolitical power and influence; weaponisation of trade, investment, and supply chains
Dominance of one state or group of states in the international system; ability to set rules and impose preferences
Geographic region where one power exercises dominant political and economic control
Mackinder's thesis that control of the Eurasian 'Heartland' (the pivot area) confers the potential for world domination; 'Who rules the Heartland commands the World-Island'
Mahan's theory that naval supremacy is the decisive factor in great power competition; control of sea lanes and chokepoints is strategically decisive
The post-2010s trend of decelerating — but not reversing — globalisation; trade and FDI growth slowing relative to GDP growth, with rising protectionism and fragmentation

State strategy of treating technological capability as a national security asset rather than a global public good; using tech policy (export controls, subsidies, standards) for geopolitical competitive advantage

Branch of geopolitics that analyses how spatial and geographic representations are constructed and used in political discourse; concerned with the symbolic meaning of space, not just its physical attributes
Post-1989 neoliberal policy framework promoting free markets, privatisation, and fiscal discipline; associated with IMF/World Bank conditionality and US-led global economic order
Alternative development model promoted by China emphasising state-led growth, political control, and economic pragmatism over Western liberal democratic norms
State that seeks to overturn or revise the existing international order rather than operate within it; Russia and China are the principal contemporary examples
Process of separating national economies from integrated global supply chains for strategic reasons; particularly relevant to US-China technology and trade competition

concepts.name: Great Power Competition
concepts.explanation: Contest for influence, resources, and strategic advantage among major states; characterises the contemporary multipolar order with US, China, and Russia as primary rivals

concepts.name: Strategic Competition for Resources
concepts.explanation: Competition for critical resources including fossil fuels, rare earth minerals, semiconductors, and control of strategic sea routes and infrastructure

concepts.name: Belt and Road Initiative (BRI)
concepts.explanation: China's massive infrastructure investment and connectivity programme spanning Asia, Africa, Europe; aims to create economic dependencies, extend influence, and build an alternative governance architecture

concepts.name: Arctic as Geopolitical Arena
concepts.explanation: Climate change opening Arctic passages and resources; US, Russia, and China competing for access, sovereignty, and influence in polar regions

concepts.name: Globalization Cycles
concepts.explanation: Globalization follows historical cycles: first wave (1815-1914), deglobalization (1920s-1950s), second wave (1950s-2010s), current slowbalization (2010s-). Each wave generates winners and losers whose political reactions shape the next phase

concepts.name: Weaponised Interdependence
concepts.explanation: Using asymmetric economic interdependence as a coercive instrument; states that occupy central network positions (in trade, finance, technology) can threaten exclusion to coerce others

concepts.name: Strategic Autonomy
concepts.explanation: Capacity of a state or bloc to act independently in foreign and security policy without excessive dependence on external actors; central concept in EU geopolitical debate post-Ukraine

distinctions.difference: Cold War: bipolar, ideological competition, nuclear deterrence, clear rules; Contemporary: multipolar, economic-technological competition, multiple dimensions (military, cyber, economic), higher risk of miscalculation

Geopolitics: power competition through territory, military, and strategic geography; Geoeconomics: power competition through economic tools — trade, investment, sanctions, supply chains — as substitutes for or complements to military force
Slowbalization: globalisation continues but at a much slower pace, with rising friction; Deglobalization: active reversal of integration as seen in the interwar period (1920s-1940s)

Washington Consensus: free markets, privatisation, fiscal discipline, liberal democracy as paired goals; Beijing Consensus: state-led development, political authoritarianism compatible with rapid growth, no liberal democratic conditionality

examples: US-China strategic competition in the Indo-Pacific
Russian use of gas exports as geopolitical leverage over Europe
Arctic resource competition and the Northern Sea Route
Belt and Road Initiative infrastructure projects across Asia, Africa, and Europe
US export controls on advanced semiconductors to China
EU debate on strategic autonomy following Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine
China declaring itself a 'near-Arctic state' despite having no Arctic coastline
Populist rise in Europe and US linked to distributional losers of globalisation

questions.question: Regarding the definition and scope of geopolitics, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Geopolitics studies how geography, territory, and spatial factors influence international relations
options.b: Traditional geopolitics emphasises how geographic characteristics shape a state's foreign policy
options.c: Critical geopolitics examines how geography is used symbolically and politically
options.d: Geopolitics is a sub-field of economics that focuses exclusively on trade route competition

questions.explanation: Geopolitics is not a sub-field of economics. It sits between International Relations and Geography, studying how spatial and geographic factors shape political power and global competition. Geoeconomics (a related concept) addresses economic dimensions but is a distinct field.

questions.question: Regarding theories of international relations relevant to geopolitics, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Realism holds that international politics is a struggle for power among self-interested states
options.b: Liberalism believes that cooperation through institutions can reduce conflict between states
options.c: Geopolitics is generally considered closer to the realist perspective, emphasising territory and power
options.d: Realism argues that international institutions are the primary mechanism for maintaining global order

questions.explanation: It is Liberalism (not Realism) that sees international institutions as primary mechanisms for order. Realism is sceptical of institutions and argues states act in self-interest regardless of institutional constraints — military power and balance of power are the realist stabilisers.

questions.question: Regarding hard power and soft power, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Hard power refers to the use of direct force — military power, coercion, and sanctions
options.b: Soft power operates through indirect influence — culture, diplomacy, and economic attraction
options.c: States use hard and soft power to contest 'territories of power' such as trade routes and natural resources
options.d: Soft power is universally more effective than hard power in achieving geopolitical objectives

questions.explanation: Neither hard nor soft power is universally superior — their effectiveness depends on context, actors, and objectives. Many geopolitical outcomes require a combination of both. Claiming one is always superior oversimplifies state strategy.

questions.question: Regarding the return of geopolitics as a concept and discipline, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Geopolitics as a discipline declined in the post-WWII era partly due to its association with Nazi and fascist thought
options.b: The term geopolitics has become increasingly popular in recent decades as great power competition intensified
options.c: The revival of geopolitical thinking reflects the return of territorial competition and power politics
options.d: The renewed interest in geopolitics confirms that geography is the sole determinant of international outcomes

questions.explanation: Geography shapes but does not solely determine outcomes — agency, ideology, technology, and institutions also matter. Geopolitics helps explain international dynamics but does not produce mechanical, deterministic results.

questions.question: Regarding imperial geopolitics and classical geopolitical thinkers, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Imperial geopolitics describes how great powers sought to extend territorial control and influence globally
options.b: Mackinder argued that control of the Eurasian 'Heartland' was key to global power
options.c: Sea power theorist Mahan argued that naval supremacy was critical to great power status
options.d: Imperial geopolitical thinking had no lasting influence on 20th or 21st century international competition

questions.explanation: Imperial geopolitical thinking has had enduring influence. The logic of controlling strategic chokepoints, trade routes, and resource-rich territories directly informs contemporary competition in the Arctic, South China Sea, and Middle East.

questions.question: Regarding post-WWI geopolitics and German Geopolitik, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: WWI cancelled several continental empires: the German, Austro-Hungarian, Ottoman, and Russian
options.b: The multipolar situation following WWI was stable due to the effective functioning of the League of Nations
options.c: Revanchist Germany and expansionist Japan developed new geopolitical strategies to justify territorial expansion
options.d: The concept of Lebensraum (vital space) was central to German geopolitical thinking in the interwar period

questions.explanation: The post-WWI order was inherently unstable. The League of Nations was weak — the US never joined, and revisionist powers (Germany, Japan) actively sought to overturn the settlement. The multipolar situation was 'bounded to quick destabilisation.'

questions.question: Regarding the Cold War geopolitical order, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: The Cold War was characterised by a bipolar structure with the US and USSR as the two superpowers
options.b: Nuclear deterrence — the threat of mutually assured destruction — played a central stabilising role
options.c: The Cold War involved only direct military confrontation between the US and USSR
options.d: Proxy wars in regions such as Korea, Vietnam, and Angola were part of Cold War competition

questions.explanation: The Cold War was notable for the absence of direct military confrontation between the superpowers, precisely because of nuclear deterrence. Competition occurred through proxy wars, ideological rivalry, the space race, arms race, and economic competition — not direct warfare.

questions.question: Regarding the post-Cold War international order, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: The immediate post-Cold War period was characterised by US unipolarity and liberal internationalism
options.b: Western states believed after the Cold War that geopolitics had been replaced by globalisation and cooperation
options.c: Russia and China accepted permanent US unipolarity without seeking to revise the international order
options.d: The return of great power competition in the 2010s challenged the assumptions of the post-Cold War liberal order

questions.explanation: Russia and China were always 'revisionist powers' seeking to revise the US-led liberal order. Russia's actions in Georgia (2008) and Ukraine (2014, 2022) and China's assertiveness in the South China Sea are clear evidence that neither accepted permanent US primacy.

questions.question: Regarding 21st century multipolarity, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Multipolarity refers to an international system with several major powers competing for influence
options.b: Geopolitical instability increases when the cost of challenging the dominant power appears lower
options.c: In a multipolar world, the degree of 'influence overlap' between major powers increases the risk of conflict
options.d: A multipolar world is inherently more stable than a bipolar one because power is distributed more evenly

questions.explanation: There is no consensus that multipolarity is inherently more stable. Some scholars argue bipolarity was actually more stable (clearer rules, deterrence). Multipolarity can increase miscalculation risk because multiple actors have overlapping interests and influence zones.

questions.question: Regarding China's strategy to seize new frontiers of power (as analysed by Elizabeth Economy in Foreign Affairs), which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: China pursues strategic dominance in emerging domains including AI, 5G infrastructure, and space
options.b: China's strategy combines economic engagement abroad with domestic political control
options.c: China's approach to global governance has fully converged with Western liberal democratic norms
options.d: China has invested in influence over international institutions and global standard-setting processes

questions.explanation: China has not adopted Western liberal norms — it has actively challenged them and promoted alternative governance models. The 'Beijing Consensus' explicitly competes with the liberal internationalist order that the West has promoted since 1945.

questions.question: Regarding Arctic geopolitics, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Climate change is opening new Arctic shipping routes including the Northern Sea Route
options.b: The Arctic contains significant reserves of oil, gas, and minerals, making it a zone of resource competition
options.c: Only Arctic coastal states (US, Russia, Canada, Norway, Denmark) have expressed strategic interest in the region
options.d: Russia has invested heavily in militarising its Arctic presence and claiming extensive continental shelf rights

questions.explanation: China, despite having no Arctic coastline, has declared itself a 'near-Arctic state' and invested in Arctic research and BRI-linked shipping routes. Non-Arctic major powers see the region as strategically important for both resources and trade routes.

questions.question: Regarding outer space as a geopolitical arena, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Outer space is no longer governed solely by states — private actors now play an increasingly significant role
options.b: Satellites provide critical infrastructure for both military operations and civilian economies
options.c: The Outer Space Treaty of 1967 provides comprehensive and effective governance for all contemporary space activities
options.d: Space has become an arena where major powers compete for supremacy in orbital capabilities and satellite systems

questions.explanation: The 1967 Treaty is widely considered inadequate for contemporary challenges including satellite militarisation, anti-satellite weapons (ASAT), orbital debris, and the explosion of private commercial space activities. Its governance framework predates the current realities.

questions.question: Regarding the geopolitics of rare earth and the energy transition, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: China controls a dominant share of rare earth production and processing, giving it significant strategic leverage
options.b: The energy transition increases geopolitical dependence on critical minerals such as lithium, cobalt, and neodymium
options.c: Transitioning to renewable energy eliminates all forms of resource-based geopolitical competition
options.d: Control over critical mineral supply chains has become a major dimension of US-China strategic competition

questions.explanation: The energy transition shifts strategic dependencies from fossil fuels to critical minerals — it does not eliminate resource competition. Countries controlling rare earth and battery mineral supplies gain new geopolitical leverage equivalent to that of oil producers in the 20th century.

questions.question: Regarding the geopolitics of fossil fuels, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Fossil fuel wealth has historically enabled energy-exporting states to translate resources into political leverage
options.b: The 'resource curse' refers to cases where resource wealth undermines institutional quality and long-term development
options.c: Fossil fuel interdependence between producers and consumers is perfectly symmetrical in its power effects
options.d: Russia used its gas exports to Europe as a geopolitical instrument, particularly vis-à-vis transit countries

questions.explanation: The interdependence is asymmetrical, not symmetrical. While consumers depend on supply, producers depend on export revenues. The side less able to absorb the shock of disruption holds less leverage, but the distribution of power varies by context. Russia's ability to cut gas supplies illustrates producer leverage.

questions.question: Regarding geoeconomics and the weaponisation of economic interdependence, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Geoeconomics refers to the use of economic tools as instruments of geopolitical power
options.b: Trade sanctions, investment restrictions, and control of supply chains are examples of geoeconomic tools
options.c: Geoeconomics represents a purely cooperative form of international relations with no coercive dimension
options.d: Control over critical minerals and technology supply chains has become a major geoeconomic battleground

questions.explanation: Geoeconomics has a strongly coercive dimension. Economic tools — sanctions, export controls, market exclusion, currency manipulation — are used to punish, pressure, and constrain rivals. It is competitive and strategic, not purely cooperative.

questions.question: Regarding geopolitics and techno-nationalism, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Techno-nationalism refers to the use of technology policy for national security and competitive advantage
options.b: The semiconductor industry has become a key battleground in US-China strategic competition
options.c: Technological knowledge was once widely treated as a global public good that states deliberately kept separate from geopolitics
options.d: US export controls on advanced chips and AI technology have no meaningful effect on the strategic balance

questions.explanation: US export controls on advanced semiconductors and AI chips are explicitly designed to constrain Chinese military and AI capabilities. Their strategic effect is significant — they delay Chinese access to frontier computing technology and force China to invest heavily in domestic substitution.

questions.question: Regarding China's Belt and Road Initiative (BRI), which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: The BRI is a large-scale infrastructure investment programme spanning Asia, Africa, and Europe
options.b: Critics argue the BRI creates economic dependencies that extend Chinese geopolitical influence
options.c: The BRI focuses exclusively on maritime trade routes and has no land component
options.d: The BRI represents a form of geoeconomic strategy combining infrastructure investment with political influence

questions.explanation: The BRI has both a maritime component (the 21st Century Maritime Silk Road) and a land component (the Silk Road Economic Belt). It explicitly covers overland routes through Central Asia and Europe as well as sea lanes through the Indian Ocean.

questions.question: Regarding the US National Security Strategy and great power competition, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: US NSS documents identify great power competition with China and Russia as primary strategic challenges
options.b: The Indo-Pacific has become a central theatre in US strategic competition with China
options.c: The US has abandoned its alliance system in favour of purely unilateral approaches to security
options.d: US strategic thinking increasingly integrates economic and technological dimensions alongside military power

questions.explanation: The US has reinvested heavily in alliances as a key pillar of its competitive strategy — reinforcing NATO in response to Russia, and building new frameworks (QUAD, AUKUS) in the Indo-Pacific to counter China. Abandoning alliances would represent the opposite of the actual strategic direction.

questions.question: Regarding Critical Geopolitics as an academic approach, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Critical Geopolitics is concerned with how geographical representations and spatial narratives shape political discourse
options.b: It examines how political actors use images of space and place to justify policy decisions
options.c: Critical Geopolitics rejects any role for actual geography in shaping politics, focusing purely on discourse
options.d: The symbolic meaning of locations — such as contested borders or capital cities — can be as politically significant as their physical characteristics

questions.explanation: Critical Geopolitics does not deny that geography shapes politics — it focuses on how geographic facts are constructed, narrated, and used. It complements rather than entirely replaces traditional geopolitics, adding a layer of discourse analysis.

questions.question: Regarding the EU's shift from geoeconomics towards geopolitics, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: The EU has historically prioritised economic integration and normative power over hard military capability
options.b: Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine accelerated EU moves towards greater strategic autonomy and defence coordination
options.c: The EU has fully transformed into a traditional geopolitical actor with military capacity equivalent to the United States
options.d: The concept of 'open strategic autonomy' reflects the EU's attempt to maintain trade openness while reducing critical strategic dependencies

questions.explanation: The EU has moved towards greater geopolitical engagement but remains far from equivalent military capacity to the US. It is primarily an economic and normative power undergoing gradual geopolitical awakening, not a fully-formed traditional military great power.

questions.question: Regarding the role of ideology in contemporary geopolitics, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: The Cold War was fundamentally an ideological contest between Western liberal democracy and Soviet communism
options.b: China promotes a 'Beijing Consensus' as an alternative development model to the Western Washington Consensus
options.c: Ideological differences between liberal democracies and autocracies have no effect on their geopolitical alignment
options.d: Whether geopolitics is driven primarily by power interests or by values remains an actively debated question in IR

questions.explanation: Ideological alignment does influence geopolitical alignment. Democracies tend to cluster in security arrangements and institutions, and autocracies show increasing coordination (e.g. Russia-China partnership). The debate is about degree, not whether ideology plays any role at all.

questions.question: Regarding the measurement of geopolitical risk, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Geopolitical risk indices attempt to quantify political instability, conflict probability, and policy uncertainty
options.b: Firms operating globally must account for geopolitical risk in investment decisions and supply chain design
options.c: Geopolitical risk is fully predictable using quantitative models, allowing accurate forecasting of conflicts
options.d: The interaction between political risk, economic policy, and security events creates complex, interconnected risks

questions.explanation: Geopolitical risk is inherently difficult to predict with precision — major events like Russia's 2022 full-scale invasion of Ukraine were seen as low-probability by most risk models until shortly before. Quantitative indices help frame and monitor risk but cannot reliably forecast specific geopolitical events.

questions.question: Regarding geopolitics and business strategy, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Geopolitical fragmentation increases supply chain risk and operational uncertainty for multinational companies
options.b: Firms operating in geopolitically contested markets face risks of sanctions, asset seizure, and sudden regulatory changes
options.c: Companies can entirely insulate their operations from geopolitical risk through sufficient diversification
options.d: The weaponisation of economic interdependence has forced firms to reconsider purely efficiency-driven globalisation strategies

questions.explanation: Diversification reduces but cannot eliminate geopolitical risk. Even highly diversified multinationals remain exposed to interstate conflicts, sanctions regimes, and sudden regulatory shifts. Complete insulation from geopolitics is not achievable for firms with global operations.

questions.question: Regarding critiques of geopolitical thinking, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Critics argue that geopolitical frameworks can oversimplify international relations by overemphasising geography
options.b: The 'against geopolitics' perspective challenges deterministic readings of geography as destiny
options.c: Critical scholars argue that geopolitical discourse can be used to justify imperialism and great power aggression
options.d: All mainstream IR scholars agree that geopolitics provides a complete and sufficient explanation of world affairs

questions.explanation: Geopolitics is actively contested in academia. Liberals, constructivists, and critical theorists argue it is insufficient and overestimates the role of geography and power, while underweighting norms, institutions, ideas, and domestic politics.

questions.question: Regarding the historical cycles of globalisation discussed in 'From Globalisation to Fragmentation', which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Globalisation has followed cyclical patterns with periods of integration succeeded by slowbalization and deglobalisation
options.b: The post-1990 wave of globalisation contributed to greater equality between countries but increased inequality within countries
options.c: The slide towards 'slowbalization' from the 2010s has no historical precedent and represents an entirely novel phenomenon
options.d: Rising populism in democratic countries has been linked to the distributional losers of economic globalisation

questions.explanation: The 2010s slowbalization has clear historical precedent. The late 19th/early 20th century showed a similar cycle: the first globalization wave (1815-1914) was followed by deglobalization in the interwar period (1920s-1950s). The current pattern mirrors this historical sequence.

questions.question: Regarding the 'multiple futures' scenarios presented in the final lecture, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Slowbalization is described as a suboptimal solution for everyone, but potentially a stable equilibrium
options.b: The 'Two Worlds Apart' scenario of bloc division would be particularly damaging for developing countries
options.c: The 'Rise of Stakeholder Geopolitics' scenario implies a return to purely realist, zero-sum competition
options.d: Historical evidence shows that deglobalization and Armageddon scenarios are not merely theoretical — they have happened before

questions.explanation: 'Stakeholder Geopolitics' is presented as a more cooperative scenario — the opposite of zero-sum realism. It implies actors recognising shared interests and managing competition through negotiation and institutions, in contrast to the purely realist dynamics that drive the other less optimistic scenarios.

questions.question: Regarding the geopolitical rivalry between Britain and Germany in the late 19th and early 20th centuries, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Germany's rapid industrialisation and naval build-up from the 1890s directly challenged British maritime supremacy
options.b: Britain and Germany competed for colonies, naval dominance, and influence across Europe and globally
options.c: Britain and Germany maintained an untroubled alliance throughout the late 19th century with no significant strategic tensions
options.d: The Anglo-German naval arms race was a major driver of the tensions that contributed to WWI

questions.explanation: Britain and Germany were in growing strategic tension from the 1890s onwards. Germany's Weltpolitik under Kaiser Wilhelm II and the construction of a High Seas Fleet explicitly designed to challenge the Royal Navy created acute anxiety in Britain. The Anglo-German naval arms race — dreadnought competition — was one of the key drivers of the alliance system breakdown that led to WWI.

questions.question: Regarding the second wave of globalisation in the 1990s-2000s, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: The 1990s-2000s saw rapid expansion of global trade, investment flows, and cross-border supply chains
options.b: China's accession to the WTO in 2001 was a pivotal moment in the integration of the global economy
options.c: The second wave of globalisation had no connection to US foreign policy or the liberal international order the US promoted
options.d: The offshoring of manufacturing to low-cost countries created economic disruption for working-class communities in developed countries

questions.explanation: The second wave of globalisation was deeply connected to US foreign policy and the liberal international order. The US used its post-Cold War dominance to build international institutions (WTO, IMF conditionality), push open markets, and embed the Washington Consensus globally. China's WTO accession was explicitly supported by the US on the premise that economic integration would promote liberalisation. Globalisation was geopolitically constructed, not spontaneous.

questions.question: Regarding the post-WWII decline of geopolitics as an academic discipline, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Geopolitics fell out of favour partly because of its association with Nazi and fascist ideology
options.b: Cold War strategic studies largely replaced geopolitics, using different vocabulary for similar concerns
options.c: Geopolitics declined because the world became fully peaceful after 1945 with no further territorial disputes
options.d: The term 'geopolitics' carried toxic connotations after Haushofer's role in providing intellectual cover for Nazism

questions.explanation: The world was far from peaceful after 1945 — the Cold War, Korea, Vietnam, decolonisation conflicts, and dozens of proxy wars followed. Geopolitics declined not because of peace but because its intellectual associations with fascism made the term politically radioactive. Cold War thinkers used different language (containment, deterrence, grand strategy) for similar analyses.

questions.question: Regarding the drivers of the contemporary revival of geopolitical thinking, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: The return of great power competition between the US, China, and Russia has made geopolitical analysis more relevant
options.b: The erosion of the post-Cold War liberal international order has prompted renewed focus on power politics
options.c: Geopolitics has revived because globalisation successfully resolved all interstate conflicts in the 1990s–2000s
options.d: Russia's 2008 Georgia war and 2014 Crimea annexation signalled the return of territorial competition

questions.explanation: Globalisation did not resolve interstate conflicts — it coincided with a period of reduced great power war but left underlying power competition intact. The revival of geopolitics reflects precisely the failure of liberal optimism: territorial conquest (Crimea, Ukraine), assertive maritime claims (South China Sea), and arms competition all demonstrate that geography and power politics never went away.

questions.question: Regarding the concept of 'territories of power' in geopolitical analysis, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Territories of power include maritime domains such as straits, sea lanes, and exclusive economic zones
options.b: In the contemporary context, cyberspace and outer space are increasingly treated as geopolitical territories
options.c: Territories of power are exclusively physical land territories controlled by nation-states
options.d: Control of strategic chokepoints such as the Strait of Hormuz or the Taiwan Strait constitutes a form of territorial power

questions.explanation: Territories of power extend far beyond physical land. Contemporary geopolitics encompasses maritime zones, airspace, cyberspace, outer space, and undersea cable infrastructure. The struggle for control over these non-land domains is a defining feature of 21st-century geopolitical competition.

questions.question: Regarding the relationship between geopolitics and globalisation, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Economic globalisation and geopolitical competition coexisted during the 1990s and 2000s
options.b: The 2010s saw growing tension between the logic of open global markets and the logic of strategic competition
options.c: Geopolitics and globalisation are permanently incompatible forces that cannot coexist in the same international system
options.d: Weaponised interdependence shows how globalisation's economic linkages can be turned into geopolitical tools

questions.explanation: Geopolitics and globalisation are not permanently incompatible — the 1990s–2000s showed they can coexist, with states pursuing both deep economic integration and strategic competition simultaneously. The concept of weaponised interdependence demonstrates that globalisation's linkages become instruments of geopolitical power rather than replacements for it.

questions.question: Regarding the origins of geopolitics as an academic discipline, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Geopolitics as a formal academic field emerged in the late 19th and early 20th centuries
options.b: Key early contributors included Rudolf Kjellén (who coined the term), Halford Mackinder, and Friedrich Ratzel
options.c: Geopolitics was first developed as an academic discipline at the University of Chicago during the Cold War
options.d: Early geopolitics was closely connected to the political and strategic concerns of European imperial powers

questions.explanation: Geopolitics was not a Cold War American invention. The term was coined by Swedish political scientist Rudolf Kjellén around 1899. The field was developed in Europe — Friedrich Ratzel in Germany, Halford Mackinder in Britain, Karl Haushofer in Germany — in the context of imperial competition. Cold War American strategists used different vocabulary (containment, deterrence) while drawing on similar geographic logic.

questions.question: Regarding the core concepts and jargon of geopolitical analysis, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Geopolitical analysis examines how space, time, culture, and politics interact to shape power
options.b: The concept of 'space' in geopolitics refers to geographic and political territory, not merely outer space
options.c: Geopolitical analysis treats geography as a static backdrop with no dynamic interaction with culture or politics
options.d: Temporal context matters in geopolitics — the same geographic feature can carry different strategic significance in different eras

questions.explanation: Geopolitics does not treat geography as a static backdrop. Space, time, and culture interact dynamically: the Arctic became strategically significant because of climate change (time); the Heartland's importance shifts with technology (railways changed it, airpower changed it further); cultural and ideological factors shape how geographic facts are interpreted politically.

questions.question: Regarding the British Empire's geopolitical strategy in the 19th century, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: British global dominance was built on naval supremacy and control of key maritime trade routes
options.b: Britain's strategy relied on a large professional continental army to project power across Europe
options.c: The Royal Navy protected British trade routes and enabled the projection of power across the global empire
options.d: Britain pursued a policy of 'splendid isolation' — avoiding permanent continental alliances while maintaining naval supremacy

questions.explanation: British power was fundamentally naval, not continental. Britain consistently avoided maintaining a large standing army and preferred to use its navy and financial power, subsidising continental allies (Prussia, Austria) to fight land wars on its behalf. This 'offshore balancing' strategy was central to British grand strategy throughout the 19th century.

questions.question: Regarding the nature of 19th-century international order, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: The 19th century is characterised as a period of apparent multipolarity with several competing great powers
options.b: The Concert of Europe (post-1815) represented an attempt to manage great power competition and avoid hegemonic war
options.c: Britain was the dominant but not unchallenged power — Germany, Russia, France, and Austria-Hungary also competed
options.d: The 19th century international order was a unipolar system with Britain as the unchallenged hegemon across all dimensions

questions.explanation: The 19th century was multipolar, not unipolar. While Britain was the leading naval and commercial power, it was not hegemonic across all dimensions. Germany led in industrial production by the 1890s; Russia dominated in land forces; France competed for colonial influence. The balance of power was managed through diplomacy and the Concert of Europe.

questions.question: Regarding the logic of 19th-century colonial expansion, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Colonial expansion was driven by a combination of economic, strategic, and ideological motivations
options.b: Control of strategic naval bases and coaling stations was a key geopolitical objective of colonial powers
options.c: Colonial expansion was driven exclusively by humanitarian motives — the 'civilising mission' — with no economic component
options.d: Access to raw materials and markets in colonial territories provided significant economic advantage to European powers

questions.explanation: Colonial expansion was driven by multiple overlapping motivations: economic (raw materials, markets, labour), strategic (naval bases, denial of resources to rivals), prestige, and ideological justifications (the 'civilising mission' or 'white man's burden'). Treating it as purely humanitarian ignores the extractive economic logic that was central to how empires actually operated.

questions.question: Regarding the structural shift from 19th-century multipolarity to post-WWII bipolarity, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: WWI and WWII destroyed the European-centred multipolar order of the 19th century
options.b: The US and USSR emerged as superpowers with a qualitatively different scale of power than previous great powers
options.c: Post-WWII bipolarity represented a continuation of the 19th-century balance of power with two new leading states
options.d: Nuclear weapons created a fundamentally new strategic logic that had no parallel in 19th-century great power competition

questions.explanation: Post-WWII bipolarity was a qualitative structural break, not a continuation. The US and USSR possessed nuclear weapons (absent from 19th-century competition), wielded ideological programmes with global appeal (liberalism vs communism), and organised blocs in entirely new ways (NATO, Warsaw Pact). The 19th-century Concert managed competition among roughly comparable powers; bipolarity was a categorically different system.

questions.question: Regarding Halford Mackinder's Heartland theory, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Mackinder argued that control of the Eurasian interior (the 'Heartland') was the key to world dominance
options.b: His famous formulation: 'Who rules the Heartland commands the World-Island; who rules the World-Island commands the world'
options.c: Mackinder identified the Western European coastal nations as the 'pivot area' of global strategic importance
options.d: Mackinder's theory explained British strategic concern about any power dominating the Eurasian landmass

questions.explanation: Mackinder identified the Eurasian interior — roughly corresponding to Russia and Central Asia — as the pivot/Heartland, not Western European coastal nations. The coastal 'Rimland' was the outer crescent. The theory was designed to warn Britain about the danger of a land power dominating the Heartland and potentially challenging British sea power.

questions.question: Regarding Alfred Thayer Mahan's sea power theory, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Mahan argued that naval supremacy was the decisive factor in great power competition
options.b: He emphasised control of sea lanes, chokepoints, and the ability to project power through maritime dominance
options.c: Mahan's ideas influenced US naval expansion and contributed to the construction of the Panama Canal
options.d: Mahan argued that land power and continental dominance were superior to sea power for achieving global hegemony

questions.explanation: Mahan argued precisely the opposite — that sea power was superior to land power for achieving global dominance. His 'The Influence of Sea Power upon History' (1890) made the case that control of the seas was the decisive factor. This directly contradicted Mackinder's land-power thesis and provided intellectual foundations for US and British naval expansion.

questions.question: Regarding Karl Haushofer's concept of Lebensraum ('vital space'), which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Lebensraum argued that states require geographic expansion to accommodate growing populations and secure resources
options.b: Haushofer had personal connections with Rudolf Hess and his ideas were used to legitimise Nazi territorial claims
options.c: The concept of Lebensraum was entirely original to Haushofer and had no precedent in earlier geographic thinking
options.d: Haushofer drew on Friedrich Ratzel's earlier concept of the state as a living organism requiring space to grow

questions.explanation: Lebensraum was not Haushofer's original invention — he developed ideas from Friedrich Ratzel's political geography (Politische Geographie, 1897), which treated states as organic entities competing for space. Haushofer systematised and politicised Ratzel's concepts, adapting them into a programme for German expansionism that directly fed into Nazi ideology.

questions.question: Regarding Nicholas Spykman's 'Rimland' concept, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Spykman modified Mackinder's framework by arguing that the Rimland (coastal regions) was more important than the Heartland
options.b: Spykman's formulation: 'Who controls the Rimland rules Eurasia; who rules Eurasia controls the destinies of the world'
options.c: Spykman's Rimland theory provided intellectual foundations for the US Cold War policy of containment
options.d: Spykman agreed with Mackinder that the Eurasian interior was strategically more important than its coastal periphery

questions.explanation: Spykman explicitly inverted Mackinder. Where Mackinder saw the Heartland (interior) as decisive, Spykman argued the Rimland — the coastal margins of Eurasia including Western Europe, the Middle East, South and East Asia — was the critical zone. Containing Soviet power in the Rimland became the geographic logic of US Cold War containment strategy.

questions.question: Regarding the Treaty of Versailles and its consequences, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: The Treaty imposed significant reparations, territorial losses, and a 'war guilt' clause on Germany
options.b: German resentment of Versailles contributed to political radicalisation and the eventual rise of Nazi revanchism
options.c: The Treaty was accepted with broad German consent as a fair settlement of WWI responsibilities
options.d: The loss of territories such as Alsace-Lorraine and German colonies fuelled nationalist grievances in the interwar period

questions.explanation: The Treaty of Versailles was deeply resented across the German political spectrum — it was widely seen as a 'Diktat' (imposed peace) rather than a negotiated settlement. The war guilt clause (Article 231), reparations, and territorial losses became central grievances exploited by nationalist and ultimately Nazi politics. There was no broad German consent.

questions.question: Regarding German Geopolitik and its relationship to National Socialism, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: German Geopolitik provided academic legitimation for Nazi expansion by supplying geographic arguments for territorial claims
options.b: Karl Haushofer had personal and professional connections with Rudolf Hess, a senior Nazi official
options.c: German Geopolitik was a purely neutral academic discipline that was misappropriated by the Nazis without any connection to the field's own logic
options.d: Concepts like Lebensraum and pan-regional spheres of influence were used to justify German expansion into Eastern Europe

questions.explanation: German Geopolitik was not a neutral academic discipline misappropriated from outside. Its leading figures including Haushofer actively promoted expansionist ideas and participated in Nazi intellectual life. While Haushofer's relationship with Nazism was complex (his son was executed for involvement in the July 1944 plot), the intellectual content of Geopolitik directly supported territorial expansion.

questions.question: Regarding Japan's expansionism in the interwar period, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Japan's expansion was driven by resource scarcity — Japan lacked domestic oil, iron ore, and other critical materials
options.b: Japan sought to establish a 'Greater East Asia Co-Prosperity Sphere' under Japanese dominance
options.c: Japan's expansion was entirely motivated by anticommunism and was welcomed by Western powers as a bulwark against the USSR
options.d: Japan's military expansion in Manchuria (1931) and China (1937) preceded its Pacific confrontation with the US

questions.explanation: Japan's expansionism was primarily resource-driven and power-seeking, not solely anticommunist. While Japan did fight the USSR (Nomonhan/Khalkhin Gol, 1939) and was part of the Anti-Comintern Pact, Western powers were not welcoming of Japanese expansion — the League of Nations condemned the Manchuria invasion and the US imposed oil embargoes precisely to constrain Japanese expansion.

questions.question: Regarding the instability of the interwar multipolar order, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: The post-WWI multipolar system proved inherently unstable, collapsing into WWII within approximately 20 years
options.b: The League of Nations failed to enforce collective security against revisionist powers
options.c: The interwar period demonstrated that multipolarity with revisionist great powers is a recipe for conflict
options.d: The Washington Naval Treaties and Locarno Agreements successfully stabilised the multipolar system and prevented WWII

questions.explanation: Neither the Washington Naval Treaties nor Locarno prevented WWII — they were temporary measures that collapsed under the pressure of revisionist expansionism. Japan withdrew from naval treaties; Germany remilitarised in violation of Versailles. The course describes the post-WWI multipolar situation as 'bounded to quick destabilisation' — and WWII broke out only 20 years after the peace settlement.

questions.question: Regarding NATO's formation and purpose, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: NATO was established in 1949 as a collective defence alliance binding the US to European security
options.b: NATO's Article 5 commits members to treat an attack on one as an attack on all
options.c: NATO was designed as a temporary organisation to be dissolved once the Berlin Crisis of 1948-49 was resolved
options.d: NATO outlasted the Cold War and continued expanding eastward after 1991

questions.explanation: NATO was not designed as a temporary crisis-response organisation. It was conceived as a permanent security architecture to deter Soviet aggression in Europe. Its durability is demonstrated by its continued expansion and relevance after the Cold War ended — it has grown from 12 to 32 members and remains active well into the 21st century.

questions.question: Regarding nuclear deterrence and the stability of Cold War bipolarity, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Mutually assured destruction (MAD) created a deterrence relationship that prevented direct US-USSR military conflict
options.b: Nuclear deterrence operated through the credible threat of catastrophic retaliation, making direct superpower war extremely costly
options.c: Nuclear deterrence prevented all military conflicts globally, including wars in Korea, Vietnam, and Afghanistan
options.d: The stability-instability paradox suggests that nuclear deterrence at the strategic level enabled more limited conflicts at lower levels

questions.explanation: Nuclear deterrence prevented direct US-USSR military confrontation but did not prevent proxy wars. Korea (1950-53), Vietnam (1955-75), and Afghanistan (1979-89) were all major military conflicts during the nuclear era. The stability-instability paradox specifically explains this: strategic nuclear stability paradoxically enabled conventional and proxy wars below the nuclear threshold.

questions.question: Regarding the nature of Cold War bipolarity, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: The Cold War order was characterised by unprecedented hostility between superpowers that never resulted in direct conflict
options.b: Bipolarity created relatively clear rules about spheres of influence that reduced the risk of accidental escalation
options.c: The Cold War was a purely bilateral affair — all other nations were passive objects of superpower competition with no agency
options.d: The approximately 50-year Cold War order, despite its tensions, proved more durable than the interwar multipolar system

questions.explanation: The Non-Aligned Movement (1961, founding members including India, Yugoslavia, Egypt) showed that many states exercised significant agency and refused to be passive objects of superpower competition. Cuba, China, and several Middle Eastern states also pursued independent courses that forced superpower adaptation. Bipolarity shaped but did not wholly determine the choices of smaller powers.

questions.question: Regarding Cold War proxy conflicts and their geopolitical logic, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Proxy wars allowed the US and USSR to compete for influence without risking direct military confrontation
options.b: Both superpowers supported allied regimes and movements in conflicts across Asia, Africa, and Latin America
options.c: Proxy conflicts were geopolitically irrelevant because they occurred only in peripheral regions of no strategic importance
options.d: The Korean War (1950-53) was an early Cold War proxy conflict where US and Soviet/Chinese interests clashed indirectly

questions.explanation: Proxy conflicts were central to Cold War geopolitics, not peripheral. Korea, Vietnam, Angola, Mozambique, Nicaragua, and Afghanistan were all contested as geopolitically significant. Each was framed by the superpowers as either a 'domino' (US) or a liberation movement (USSR) — the logic of ideological and strategic competition extended globally, not just to 'core' areas.

questions.question: Regarding Francis Fukuyama's 'End of History' thesis, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Fukuyama argued that liberal democracy had won the ideological contest with communism after the Cold War
options.b: The thesis anticipated convergence toward liberal democratic capitalism as the dominant form of governance
options.c: Fukuyama predicted that geopolitical competition would intensify after 1989 as new ideological rivals emerged to challenge liberal democracy
options.d: The 'End of History' framing was challenged by the subsequent rise of China, resurgent Russia, and political Islam as alternatives

questions.explanation: Fukuyama predicted the opposite of intensified competition — he argued history as ideological struggle had ended because liberal democracy had no remaining serious rival. His thesis is notable precisely because it expected convergence, not new challengers. The subsequent rise of China's authoritarian capitalism and Russia's revanchism directly falsified this expectation.

questions.question: Regarding post-1989 NATO expansion, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: NATO expanded eastward after 1991, admitting former Warsaw Pact states including Poland, Hungary, and the Czech Republic
options.b: Russia consistently opposed NATO expansion as a threat to its security interests
options.c: A formal, legally binding treaty committed the US and NATO never to expand eastward after German reunification
options.d: The debate about whether informal assurances were made to the Soviet leadership about NATO expansion remains contested

questions.explanation: No formal, legally binding treaty committing NATO not to expand eastward was ever signed. The debate centres on informal discussions and verbal assurances during German reunification negotiations in 1990. These were political conversations, not treaty commitments — a distinction Russia disputes but Western governments maintain. The 1997 NATO-Russia Founding Act was the closest thing to a formal arrangement.

questions.question: Regarding the Washington Consensus and post-Cold War economic policy, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: The Washington Consensus promoted free markets, privatisation, and fiscal discipline as the development model
options.b: IMF and World Bank conditionality programmes applied Washington Consensus prescriptions across the developing world
options.c: The Washington Consensus promoted state-led development, capital controls, and protectionism as its core policy tools
options.d: The Washington Consensus is associated with the era of US-led liberal international order following the Cold War

questions.explanation: State-led development, capital controls, and protectionism are precisely what the Washington Consensus opposed. Its ten policy prescriptions (Williamson, 1989) included fiscal discipline, trade liberalisation, privatisation, deregulation, and property rights protection. These are associated with the Beijing Consensus, not the Washington Consensus.

questions.question: Regarding US unipolarity in the 1990s-2000s, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: The 1990s represented an unprecedented moment of US dominance across military, economic, and cultural dimensions
options.b: US unipolarity was used to expand liberal institutions globally — NATO, WTO, IMF — under American leadership
options.c: US engagement with China, including supporting WTO accession, was premised on the belief that economic integration would promote political liberalisation
options.d: US unipolarity permanently prevented the emergence of any rival great power, making China's rise impossible to explain

questions.explanation: US unipolarity did not permanently prevent great power emergence. The engagement strategy with China — including WTO accession (2001) — accelerated Chinese economic growth and technological development. US dominance provided a permissive environment for China's rise rather than blocking it. The 2007-8 financial crisis then accelerated the relative shift in power.

questions.question: Regarding the 2007-8 global financial crisis as a geopolitical turning point, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: The crisis hit Western economies severely, undermining confidence in the Washington Consensus model
options.b: China's continued strong growth through the crisis shifted relative perceptions of US and Chinese economic power
options.c: The crisis had no geopolitical consequences and reinforced confidence in US-led liberal economic institutions
options.d: The crisis contributed to a legitimacy deficit for Western-led multilateral institutions like the IMF and World Bank

questions.explanation: The 2007-8 crisis was geopolitically transformative, not reinforcing of the status quo. It exposed vulnerabilities in the US financial model, discredited Washington Consensus prescriptions, emboldened revisionist narratives about Western decline, and accelerated China's rise in relative terms. The G20 replacing the G7 as the primary forum for global economic governance was one institutional consequence.

questions.question: Regarding China's development model and its geopolitical implications, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: China's rapid development combined state direction with gradual market opening, diverging from Washington Consensus prescriptions
options.b: China's economic rise translated into increased military spending and more assertive foreign policy after the mid-2000s
options.c: China's development model required immediate privatisation and liberalisation of all sectors to succeed
options.d: The Chinese model's success challenged the assumption that economic development requires liberal democratic political institutions

questions.explanation: China's model was explicitly state-directed, retaining state ownership in strategic sectors while gradually opening others to market forces. China's experience directly contradicted the Washington Consensus prescriptions of rapid privatisation and liberalisation. The Beijing Consensus emerged precisely as an alternative model showing that state capitalism could achieve rapid development without political democratisation.

questions.question: Regarding the rise of regional powers in the 21st century, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: The relative decline of US unipolarity created space for regional powers to assert more autonomous foreign policies
options.b: States such as Iran, Turkey, Saudi Arabia, and India have pursued increasingly independent strategies in their regions
options.c: Regional powers in Asia and the Middle East have uniformly aligned with the US in balancing against China
options.d: The multipolar shift involves not just China as a rival to the US but a wider diffusion of power to multiple regional actors

questions.explanation: Regional powers have pursued diverse hedging strategies rather than uniformly aligning with the US. India hedges between the US and Russia; Turkey pursues an autonomous course within NATO; Saudi Arabia and the UAE have cultivated economic relationships with China while maintaining US security ties. The multipolar diffusion of power means fewer states feel compelled to fully align with any single great power.

questions.question: Regarding China's strategy in multilateral institutions (as analysed by Elizabeth Economy), which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: China has sought to increase its influence within existing international institutions like the UN system and IMF
options.b: China created parallel institutions (AIIB, New Development Bank) to supplement its influence in global governance
options.c: China has systematically withdrawn from multilateral institutions and opposes all forms of international cooperation
options.d: China has pursued leadership positions in specialised UN agencies including the ITU, FAO, and ICAO

questions.explanation: China has done the opposite of withdrawing from multilateralism — it has strategically engaged with and shaped multilateral institutions. China simultaneously works within existing institutions (increasing IMF voting share, leading UN agencies) and creates alternative ones (AIIB, BRI). This is an inside-outside strategy, not withdrawal.

questions.question: Regarding China's push to dominate 5G infrastructure globally, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Huawei and ZTE became dominant global suppliers of 5G equipment through a combination of state subsidies and competitive pricing
options.b: Western governments raised concerns that Chinese 5G equipment could provide Beijing with intelligence access or a kill switch over critical infrastructure
options.c: 5G infrastructure dominance is exclusively a commercial advantage with no geopolitical implications
options.d: The US-led campaign to exclude Huawei from allied 5G networks reflects the intersection of technology and geopolitical competition

questions.explanation: 5G infrastructure dominance has direct geopolitical implications. Who builds a country's 5G network potentially has access to data flows and the ability to disrupt communications. China's push through Huawei is part of its techno-nationalist strategy to set global standards and establish strategic leverage through infrastructure — explicitly a geopolitical, not merely commercial, objective.

questions.question: Regarding China's approach to technology acquisition and innovation, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: China has used multiple channels to acquire foreign technology including joint ventures, academic exchange, and corporate acquisitions
options.b: Market access requirements forcing technology transfer were a significant mechanism of Chinese technology acquisition
options.c: China relies exclusively on domestic R&D for all technological development, never seeking to acquire foreign knowledge
options.d: Made in China 2025 reflected China's ambition to achieve dominance in 10 strategic technology sectors

questions.explanation: China has used a wide range of technology acquisition channels beyond domestic R&D: mandatory technology transfer through joint venture requirements, strategic acquisition of foreign technology companies, academic and research talent programmes, and intelligence operations. This multi-channel approach is why the US imposed restrictions on Chinese investment in technology companies and academic cooperation.

questions.question: Regarding China's governance model and its international promotion, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: China promotes its development model as an alternative for countries seeking growth without liberal democratic conditionality
options.b: China has used platforms like the UN Human Rights Council to defend state sovereignty against Western human rights interventionism
options.c: China explicitly demands that all BRI partner countries adopt the Chinese one-party political system as a condition for infrastructure financing
options.d: The Beijing Consensus contrasts with the Washington Consensus by separating economic development from democratic governance requirements

questions.explanation: China does not require adoption of its political system as an explicit condition for BRI or other economic cooperation — this is one feature that distinguishes it from Western development aid with governance conditionality. China promotes non-interference in internal political affairs, which is itself an ideological position. Many authoritarian governments find this 'no strings' approach attractive precisely because it imposes no political requirements.

questions.question: Regarding the Northern Sea Route (NSR) and its strategic significance, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: The Northern Sea Route connects Europe and Asia through Arctic waters north of Russia, potentially shortening shipping distances significantly
options.b: Climate change and melting Arctic ice have made the NSR more navigable, increasing its attractiveness as a commercial route
options.c: The Northern Sea Route is already fully operational year-round, providing a reliable alternative to the Suez Canal for most shippers
options.d: Russia seeks to control and profit from the NSR, treating it as a national waterway and charging transit fees

questions.explanation: The NSR remains seasonal and operationally challenging — it requires icebreaker escort for much of the year, carries high insurance costs, lacks adequate rescue and port infrastructure, and is only navigable without icebreaker assistance for a limited summer window. While growing in use, it is not a year-round reliable alternative to the Suez Canal, which handles far greater volumes.

questions.question: Regarding Arctic sovereignty claims and governance, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: The five Arctic coastal states — Russia, Canada, US, Norway, and Denmark (via Greenland) — have overlapping continental shelf claims
options.b: Russia has invested heavily in Arctic militarisation including reopening Cold War-era Arctic bases
options.c: All Arctic sovereignty disputes have been resolved through the Arctic Council and no further claims are pending
options.d: The UN Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS) provides the framework for adjudicating Arctic continental shelf claims

questions.explanation: Arctic sovereignty disputes are not resolved. Russia's extended continental shelf claim over the Lomonosov Ridge is disputed by Canada and Denmark. The CLCS (Commission on the Limits of the Continental Shelf) process is ongoing. China, while not an Arctic state, also contests aspects of Arctic governance and has interests in the region as a 'near-Arctic state.'

questions.question: Regarding China's role in Arctic geopolitics, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: China has designated itself a 'near-Arctic state' despite having no Arctic coastline
options.b: China has invested in Arctic research stations, icebreakers, and has pursued scientific cooperation in the region
options.c: China has no interest in the Arctic because its geographic distance makes any involvement logistically impossible
options.d: China sees the Arctic as part of a 'Polar Silk Road' extension of the Belt and Road Initiative

questions.explanation: China has been actively and deliberately pursuing Arctic interests despite its geographic distance — investing in research stations, purchasing icebreakers, securing observer status in the Arctic Council, and framing Arctic involvement as part of its BRI 'Polar Silk Road.' China's interests are resource access, shipping route development, and strategic positioning, none of which are logistically impossible from China.

questions.question: Regarding Arctic resource competition, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: The Arctic is estimated to contain approximately 30% of the world's undiscovered natural gas reserves
options.b: Melting Arctic ice is making previously inaccessible hydrocarbon and mineral deposits more reachable
options.c: All major Arctic oil and gas reserves have already been extracted, so resource competition is no longer a driver of Arctic geopolitics
options.d: Arctic resource development creates tensions between economic opportunity for Arctic states and environmental concerns

questions.explanation: Arctic resource development is in its early stages, not complete. Estimates suggest the Arctic holds ~30% of undiscovered global gas and ~13% of undiscovered oil. As ice retreats, these become more accessible and economically viable. Resource competition is a growing, not past, driver of Arctic geopolitical interest — particularly as fossil fuel demand persists.

questions.question: Regarding anti-satellite (ASAT) weapons and space security, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Russia, China, the US, and India have all tested anti-satellite weapons capable of destroying satellites in orbit
options.b: ASAT tests create space debris that can threaten other satellites and the International Space Station
options.c: The 1967 Outer Space Treaty explicitly prohibits the development and testing of anti-satellite weapons
options.d: Targeting an adversary's satellites in conflict would disrupt GPS, communications, intelligence, and financial systems simultaneously

questions.explanation: The Outer Space Treaty of 1967 prohibits placing weapons of mass destruction in orbit and bans military installations on celestial bodies — but it does not explicitly prohibit ASAT weapons. This is a major governance gap. All four countries mentioned have conducted ASAT tests in breach of no specific treaty obligation, though India's 2019 test was widely condemned for the debris it created.

questions.question: Regarding the role of private actors in space geopolitics, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: SpaceX's Starlink constellation has provided Ukraine with critical satellite communications during the war with Russia
options.b: Private space companies like SpaceX, Amazon, and OneWeb are launching thousands of satellites, transforming the orbital environment
options.c: Private sector space actors are completely separate from state power and have no geopolitical implications
options.d: The commercialisation of space has blurred the line between civilian and military space infrastructure

questions.explanation: The Starlink-Ukraine example demonstrates that private space assets are deeply entangled with state power and geopolitics. SpaceX provided Ukraine with a decisive communications advantage — a private company became a significant military-strategic actor. When Elon Musk reportedly restricted Starlink coverage near Crimea, it demonstrated the geopolitical power of private space infrastructure operators.

questions.question: Regarding satellite navigation systems as geopolitical tools, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Multiple countries have developed independent satellite navigation systems to reduce dependence on US-operated GPS
options.b: Russia (GLONASS), China (BeiDou), and the EU (Galileo) all operate independent navigation constellations
options.c: GPS is the only functioning satellite navigation system globally, as all other systems remain experimental
options.d: Independent navigation systems allow states to maintain military and commercial precision capabilities without relying on potential adversaries

questions.explanation: GPS is far from the only functioning navigation system. Russia's GLONASS has been operational since the Soviet era; China's BeiDou reached global coverage in 2020; the EU's Galileo is operational; India's NavIC covers the subcontinent. These systems were explicitly developed to reduce dependence on US-controlled GPS, which the US can theoretically degrade or deny to adversaries.

questions.question: Regarding the governance of outer space, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: The 1967 Outer Space Treaty prohibits placing nuclear weapons or other WMD in Earth orbit
options.b: The Outer Space Treaty designates outer space as the 'province of all mankind' and prohibits national appropriation
options.c: The 1967 Outer Space Treaty provides comprehensive and fully adequate governance for all 21st-century space activities including commercial operations, debris, and ASAT weapons
options.d: The proliferation of commercial satellites and ASAT capabilities has created significant governance gaps in the existing space law framework

questions.explanation: The 1967 Treaty is widely regarded as wholly inadequate for contemporary space realities. It predates commercial space, orbital debris problems, ASAT weapons, lunar resource extraction, and the explosion of private actors. It does not address debris, does not prohibit ASAT, and its provisions on 'non-appropriation' are increasingly contested by companies and states seeking to profit from space resources.

questions.question: Regarding China's dominance in rare earth production and processing, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: China controls a dominant share (roughly 60-85%) of global rare earth mining and an even higher share of processing
options.b: China has demonstrated willingness to use rare earth export restrictions as a geopolitical tool, as against Japan in 2010
options.c: Rare earths are so widely distributed globally that China's dominance could be easily and quickly substituted
options.d: Rare earth elements are critical inputs for electric vehicles, wind turbines, defence electronics, and semiconductor manufacturing

questions.explanation: While rare earths are found in many locations globally, the processing infrastructure is the bottleneck — China dominates rare earth chemical processing even more than mining. Developing alternative mining and processing capacity requires years of investment and permitting. The 2010 Japan episode showed that China can impose severe short-term supply disruptions even if long-term substitution is possible.

questions.question: Regarding critical minerals for the energy transition, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Lithium is concentrated in the 'Lithium Triangle' of Chile, Argentina, and Bolivia, plus significant deposits in Australia
options.b: Cobalt, essential for lithium-ion batteries, is predominantly mined in the Democratic Republic of Congo
options.c: Critical minerals for the energy transition are evenly distributed globally, creating no new geopolitical dependencies
options.d: The geographic concentration of critical minerals creates new strategic vulnerabilities analogous to oil dependence in the fossil fuel era

questions.explanation: Critical minerals are among the most geographically concentrated resources. Lithium: three countries supply ~80% of global production; Cobalt: DRC produces ~70%; Rare earths: China processes ~85%. This concentration is, if anything, higher than oil, creating significant new dependencies for countries pursuing electrification without domestic mineral resources.

questions.question: Regarding the energy transition and its effect on fossil fuel producers, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: The energy transition creates long-term structural challenges for petrostates dependent on oil and gas revenues
options.b: The concept of 'petrostates vs electrostates' captures the diverging geopolitical positions of fossil fuel vs mineral-rich states
options.c: The global energy transition eliminates all geopolitical leverage of fossil fuel producing states immediately upon adoption of renewables
options.d: Some petrostates are attempting to diversify their economies ahead of declining fossil fuel demand (e.g. Saudi Vision 2030)

questions.explanation: The transition is gradual — fossil fuels remain the dominant energy source globally and will for decades. Petrostates retain significant leverage during the transition period, and events like the 2022 energy crisis showed how rapidly supply disruptions re-expose dependency. The transition shifts the long-term trajectory but does not immediately eliminate fossil fuel geopolitical leverage.

questions.question: Regarding Western policy responses to critical mineral supply chain risks, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: The US Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) included incentives for domestic critical mineral production and processing
options.b: 'Friend-shoring' refers to redirecting supply chains toward geopolitically aligned partner countries
options.c: All Western governments responded to critical mineral risks by nationalising foreign-owned mines and mineral assets
options.d: The EU's Critical Raw Materials Act aimed to reduce European dependence on single-source critical mineral suppliers

questions.explanation: Nationalisation of foreign mineral assets has not been the Western response strategy.
