Western governments have used incentives (IRA subsidies), trade agreements, investment facilitation with partner countries (friend-shoring), strategic stockpiling, and R&D for material substitution. Nationalising foreign assets would breach trade agreements and undermine the rule-of-law principles Western governments promote.

questions.question: Regarding the 1973 Arab oil embargo, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: The embargo was triggered by US and Dutch support for Israel during the Yom Kippur War
options.b: The embargo created three simultaneous crises: geopolitical, energy market, and US domestic political (Watergate)
options.c: The 1973 embargo was universally applied against all Western nations without distinction
options.d: The Arab OPEC oil embargo formally ended on March 18, 1974

questions.explanation: The embargo was targeted, not universal. Arab OPEC members specifically targeted the US and Netherlands for their pro-Israel stance while other countries received different treatment. This selective application demonstrated how energy exports can be used as a targeted geopolitical weapon rather than a blunt instrument applied indiscriminately.

questions.question: Regarding the US shale revolution and its geopolitical consequences, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: By December 2018, shale gas accounted for approximately 70% of US natural gas production
options.b: The shale revolution made the US the world's largest oil and natural gas producer by the mid-2010s
options.c: The shale revolution had no effect on US foreign policy because domestic energy production is irrelevant to geopolitics
options.d: US shale production pressured both Saudi Arabia (oil) and Russia (gas), altering the global energy balance

questions.explanation: The shale revolution had profound geopolitical consequences. Energy independence reduced US dependence on Gulf states, enabling the 'Pivot to Asia' without fear of retaliatory energy cutoffs. US LNG exports allowed European allies to reduce Russian gas dependence. Saudi Arabia's market share strategy (2014 oil price collapse) was partly aimed at making US shale uneconomical. Energy production is deeply intertwined with geopolitics.

questions.question: Regarding EU dependence on Russian natural gas before 2022, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: In 2021, the EU imported approximately 155 billion cubic metres of gas from Russia, representing about 40% of EU supply
options.b: The 'Wandel durch Handel' (change through trade) paradigm held that economic integration would moderate Russia's behaviour
options.c: EU gas dependence on Russia created no geopolitical vulnerability because energy trade is always mutually beneficial
options.d: After Russia's 2022 invasion, Norway accounted for ~30% of EU pipeline gas imports while the US supplied ~50% of EU LNG

questions.explanation: EU gas dependence on Russia was a significant geopolitical vulnerability, as the 2022 energy crisis demonstrated. Russia weaponised gas supplies by cutting pipeline flows, triggering energy price spikes across Europe. The assumption of mutual benefit proved wrong — Russia was willing to accept economic damage from lost revenues to use energy as strategic leverage. 'Wandel durch Handel' collapsed as a policy paradigm.

questions.question: Regarding the energy trilemma concept, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: The energy trilemma identifies three competing goals: energy security, energy equity (affordability/access), and sustainability
options.b: Pursuing maximum energy security (domestic fossil fuels) may conflict with sustainability goals
options.c: The energy trilemma holds that any well-designed energy policy can fully achieve all three goals simultaneously without trade-offs
options.d: The tension between the three goals means energy policy requires explicit prioritisation and acceptance of trade-offs

questions.explanation: The trilemma framework explicitly recognises that the three goals are in fundamental tension — achieving all three fully simultaneously is not possible. Maximum security (domestic coal/gas) conflicts with sustainability; cheapest energy (equity) often means fossil fuels; rapid decarbonisation (sustainability) can hurt affordability and challenge supply security during transition. Policy requires choosing priorities.

questions.question: Regarding the concept of weaponised interdependence, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Weaponised interdependence refers to using asymmetric economic linkages as instruments of coercion
options.b: States occupying central 'hub' positions in global networks (trade, finance, technology) can threaten to exclude others
options.c: Weaponised interdependence is most effective when economic dependence is symmetrical between the two parties
options.d: The US use of dollar-denominated financial systems to impose sanctions is a key example of weaponised interdependence

questions.explanation: Weaponised interdependence works through asymmetry, not symmetry. The party that is more central to the network (and whose exclusion the other can less afford) holds leverage. Symmetrical dependence actually reduces coercive capacity because both sides face equivalent pain from disruption. The theory (Farrell & Newman) specifically stresses asymmetric network positions as the source of coercive power.

questions.question: Regarding dollar dominance and its geopolitical significance, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: The US dollar's role as the world's primary reserve currency gives the US unique geopolitical leverage
options.b: Control of SWIFT and dollar clearing allows the US to impose secondary sanctions affecting non-US financial actors
options.c: Dollar dominance is a purely economic phenomenon with no geopolitical implications for states or firms
options.d: Russia and China have taken steps to develop alternative payment systems to reduce dollar dependence

questions.explanation: Dollar dominance is a primary geopolitical tool. The US can exclude any country or financial institution from dollar clearing, effectively cutting it off from global trade. Secondary sanctions extend this power to non-US firms that do business with sanctioned entities. This is why states like Russia and China are actively developing alternatives (CIPS, bilateral currency swap lines) to reduce their exposure to US financial coercion.

questions.question: Regarding the use of economic sanctions as geoeconomic tools, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Economic sanctions seek to change target state behaviour through economic pressure rather than military force
options.b: The US-led sanctions regime against Russia following the 2022 invasion was the broadest Western sanctions package in history
options.c: Academic research consistently shows that sanctions always achieve their political objectives within a short timeframe
options.d: Secondary sanctions can affect third-country firms that do business with sanctioned entities, extending US reach beyond its borders

questions.explanation: The academic literature is actually quite sceptical about sanctions effectiveness. Studies suggest that sanctions achieve their stated political objectives only in a minority of cases, particularly when targeted states can access alternatives (China-Russia trade expansion post-2022) or are willing to absorb significant economic pain. Sanctions impose costs but rarely force policy reversals by determined authoritarian states.

questions.question: Regarding geoeconomics and classical free trade economics, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Classical free trade theory (Ricardo) argues that open trade benefits all parties through comparative advantage
options.b: Geoeconomics prioritises strategic and distributional effects of trade over aggregate efficiency gains
options.c: Geoeconomics and classical free trade economics are fully compatible — both support open markets as the optimal state policy
options.d: The geoeconomic perspective justifies industrial policy, export controls, and investment restrictions that classical economics would oppose

questions.explanation: Geoeconomics directly challenges classical free trade economics. Where free trade theory argues that open markets maximise welfare for all parties, geoeconomics argues that the strategic and distributional effects of who controls which industries, supply chains, and technologies matter as much as aggregate efficiency. Geoeconomic logic justifies subsidising strategic sectors, restricting technology exports, and blocking foreign acquisitions — all of which classical economics would consider welfare-reducing.

questions.question: Regarding Taiwan's TSMC and its geopolitical significance, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: TSMC manufactures the vast majority of the world's most advanced semiconductor chips (below 5nm)
options.b: TSMC's concentration in Taiwan makes it a central flashpoint in US-China strategic competition
options.c: Advanced semiconductor manufacturing capacity is evenly distributed globally, making TSMC's Taiwan location strategically irrelevant
options.d: A Chinese seizure of TSMC would deliver critical technology advantage while denying it to Western defence and technology industries

questions.explanation: Advanced semiconductor manufacturing is extremely concentrated, not distributed. TSMC alone produces ~90% of chips below 7nm; Samsung produces most of the rest. Neither the US, EU, nor Japan currently has competitive capacity at the leading edge. This extreme concentration makes Taiwan's semiconductor industry — and the geopolitical question of Taiwan's status — central to 21st-century technology competition.

questions.question: Regarding the US CHIPS and Science Act (2022), which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: The CHIPS Act provided approximately $52 billion in direct subsidies to incentivise semiconductor manufacturing in the United States
options.b: The CHIPS Act represents a significant adoption of industrial policy in contradiction to free market economic principles
options.c: The CHIPS Act was designed to reduce US dependence on Asian semiconductor manufacturing for strategic national security reasons
options.d: The CHIPS Act's primary purpose was to reduce corporate taxes for technology companies operating abroad

questions.explanation: The CHIPS Act was not primarily a tax cut — it was direct industrial policy: subsidies and incentives to build semiconductor fabs on US soil. Companies like TSMC, Intel, and Samsung received federal subsidies to construct US manufacturing facilities. Its purpose was explicitly geostrategic: reducing dependence on Asian (particularly Taiwanese) chip production for national security reasons.

questions.question: Regarding artificial intelligence and geopolitical competition, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: AI capability has become a central dimension of US-China strategic competition, alongside conventional and nuclear military power
options.b: The US imposed export controls restricting China's access to advanced AI chips (Nvidia H100, A100) for national security reasons
options.c: AI is geopolitically neutral because algorithms can be freely shared across borders without strategic consequences
options.d: Military applications of AI — autonomous weapons, intelligence analysis, logistics optimisation — create direct national security stakes

questions.explanation: AI is far from geopolitically neutral. Advanced AI requires high-performance chips (subject to US export controls), massive data (raising sovereignty questions), and specialised talent (subject to visa restrictions). The military applications — autonomous weapons, surveillance, cyber operations, decision-support — make AI a core national security technology. The US explicitly cites AI as a primary reason for semiconductor export controls against China.

questions.question: Regarding technology standards as a dimension of geopolitical competition, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Who sets technical standards for emerging technologies (5G, AI, IoT) gains commercial advantage and can shape who benefits from the technology
options.b: China has been actively pursuing leadership positions in international standards-setting bodies including the ITU
options.c: Technical standards are purely engineering decisions with no commercial or geopolitical implications
options.d: The contest over 5G standards involved both technical and political dimensions, with Western countries concerned about Chinese dominance

questions.explanation: Technical standards have profound commercial and geopolitical implications. Setting the standard for a technology determines which products are compatible, which companies win market share, and who can be excluded. Chinese leadership in ITU standards for video compression (HEVC alternatives) and 5G network architecture directly translates into commercial advantages for Chinese firms and potential strategic leverage over networks built to Chinese specifications.

questions.question: Regarding the 'debt trap diplomacy' critique of BRI, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: The debt trap hypothesis argues that China deliberately designs BRI loans to be unpayable, enabling seizure of strategic assets
options.b: The Hambantota port in Sri Lanka is frequently cited as an example of debt-for-equity swap under Chinese pressure
options.c: Academic research has fully confirmed that debt trap diplomacy is China's systematic and deliberate strategy across all BRI projects
options.d: Many researchers argue that BRI debt problems reflect poor project selection and governance rather than deliberate entrapment

questions.explanation: Academic research has not confirmed debt trap diplomacy as a systematic deliberate strategy. Studies by AidData, Johns Hopkins, and others find that BRI problems often reflect poor project planning, corruption, and local political decisions rather than a coordinated Chinese entrapment strategy. While some cases (Hambantota) show concerning patterns, the evidence for a deliberate system-wide debt trap strategy is contested.

questions.question: Regarding the geographic scope of the Belt and Road Initiative, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: BRI has both a land component (Silk Road Economic Belt) and a maritime component (21st Century Maritime Silk Road)
options.b: BRI projects span Asia, Africa, Latin America, and extend into Europe including EU member states
options.c: The BRI operates exclusively within Asia and has no presence in African or European countries
options.d: Greece's Piraeus port and multiple Central/Eastern European countries have participated in Chinese BRI-related investment

questions.explanation: BRI extends well beyond Asia — it explicitly covers Africa (major infrastructure investments in many countries), Europe (Piraeus port, the former 17+1 format with Central/Eastern European countries), and Latin America. The maritime silk road connects Chinese ports through the Indian Ocean to East Africa and Europe. BRI is a truly global infrastructure strategy.

questions.question: Regarding Western alternatives to the BRI, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: The EU's Global Gateway programme (€300 billion) was explicitly designed as a quality alternative to BRI-style investment
options.b: The G7's Partnership for Global Infrastructure and Investment (PGII) represents a coordinated Western response to Chinese connectivity initiatives
options.c: Western democracies have made no attempt to develop competitive infrastructure investment alternatives to counter BRI
options.d: Japan's Quality Infrastructure Initiative also competes with BRI by emphasising transparency and environmental/labour standards

questions.explanation: Western governments have developed multiple competitive responses to BRI: the EU's Global Gateway (€300bn), the G7's PGII, the US-Japan-Australia Blue Dot Network, and Japan's Quality Infrastructure Initiative. All explicitly position themselves as transparent, governance-compliant alternatives to BRI. The claim that no response exists is factually wrong.

questions.question: Regarding BRI governance and contract terms, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: BRI contracts have been criticised for confidentiality clauses preventing public disclosure of terms
options.b: Some BRI agreements have included provisions for Chinese arbitration rather than international dispute resolution
options.c: BRI project agreements are fully transparent public documents that comply with all international best practices in development finance
options.d: The lack of BRI transparency contrasts with standards set by multilateral development banks like the World Bank and ADB

questions.explanation: BRI contracts are frequently criticised for opacity, not transparency. AidData's research found that many BRI loan agreements contain confidentiality clauses, collateralisation provisions (pledging sovereign assets), and dispute resolution terms favouring China. This diverges significantly from World Bank or ADB standards, which require public disclosure, environmental/social safeguards, and internationally recognised dispute resolution.

questions.question: Regarding AUKUS and Indo-Pacific security architecture, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: AUKUS is a trilateral security partnership between Australia, the United Kingdom, and the United States
options.b: A central element of AUKUS is the transfer of nuclear-powered submarine technology to Australia
options.c: AUKUS is primarily a trade agreement focused on digital technology and market access
options.d: AUKUS represents a significant US and UK strategic commitment to Australia's conventional deterrence capability in the Indo-Pacific

questions.explanation: AUKUS is explicitly a security pact, not a trade agreement. Its headline commitment is the transfer of nuclear-powered submarine technology — allowing Australia to field submarines with far greater range and stealth than conventional diesel-electric boats. It also covers advanced capabilities in AI, quantum, and cyber. It is directly aimed at the Indo-Pacific maritime balance vis-à-vis China.

questions.question: Regarding the Quadrilateral Security Dialogue (QUAD), which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: The QUAD comprises the United States, Japan, Australia, and India
options.b: India's participation in the QUAD is notable given its historically non-aligned foreign policy tradition
options.c: The QUAD is a formal military alliance with a binding mutual defence treaty equivalent to NATO's Article 5
options.d: The QUAD focuses on security cooperation across the Indo-Pacific including maritime security, technology, and supply chains

questions.explanation: The QUAD is not a formal alliance — it has no binding mutual defence treaty. It is a security forum and policy coordination mechanism. India in particular has resisted formalisation into a military alliance. This distinguishes it from NATO and reflects India's preference for maintaining strategic autonomy while cooperating with partners on specific shared interests.

questions.question: Regarding US policy toward Taiwan, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: The US maintains a 'One China Policy' acknowledging (but not endorsing) the Chinese position that Taiwan is part of China
options.b: The Taiwan Relations Act (1979) commits the US to provide Taiwan with defensive arms
options.c: The US has a formal treaty commitment to defend Taiwan with military force in the event of a Chinese invasion
options.d: US 'strategic ambiguity' on Taiwan defence is deliberately maintained to deter both Chinese aggression and Taiwanese unilateral independence declaration

questions.explanation: The US does not have a formal treaty commitment to defend Taiwan — the 1979 Taiwan Relations Act provides for arms sales but deliberately stops short of a binding defence guarantee. 'Strategic ambiguity' means the US neither confirms nor denies it would intervene militarily. This ambiguity is intentional: it deters China from assuming the US won't intervene while discouraging Taiwan from declaring independence assuming the US will.

questions.question: Regarding the integration of economic and technological tools in US national security strategy, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Contemporary US strategy integrates economic, technological, and military tools as complementary instruments of competition
options.b: Export controls on advanced semiconductors are explicitly framed in US NSS documents as a national security tool
options.c: US national security strategy treats economic and technological competition as entirely separate from military strategy
options.d: The integration of investment screening (CFIUS), export controls, and alliance coordination reflects the whole-of-government approach to great power competition

questions.explanation: Post-2017 US National Security Strategy explicitly treats economic competition, technology, and military power as integrated and inseparable. The concept of 'integrated deterrence' (Biden era) and 'America First' economic nationalism (Trump era) both, in different ways, link economic and technological tools to security strategy. CFIUS investment screening, export controls, and sanctions are all explicitly national security instruments.

questions.question: Regarding the founding intellectual traditions of Critical Geopolitics, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Critical Geopolitics draws on poststructuralist theory and discourse analysis rather than traditional power-politics frameworks
options.b: Key figures include Gearóid Ó Tuathail (Gerard Toal), Simon Dalby, and John Agnew
options.c: Critical Geopolitics emerged from the University of Chicago School of Economics and focuses on rational-choice models of spatial competition
options.d: Critical Geopolitics is concerned with how geographic knowledge and spatial representations are produced and politically deployed

questions.explanation: Critical Geopolitics has no connection to the Chicago School of Economics. It emerged from political geography and draws on poststructuralism, discourse theory, and critical social science. Ó Tuathail, Dalby, and Agnew developed it as a critique of both traditional geopolitics and international relations, asking how geographic representations are constructed rather than treating them as natural facts.

questions.question: Regarding the distinction between 'formal,' 'practical,' and 'popular' geopolitics, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: 'Formal' geopolitics refers to academic and intellectual discourse about space and international relations
options.b: 'Practical' geopolitics refers to the geographical reasoning embedded in foreign policy and state discourse
options.c: 'Popular' geopolitics examines how geographic ideas are reproduced through media, film, and popular culture
options.d: Critical Geopolitics argues that formal, practical, and popular geopolitics always produce identical representations of the same geographic realities

questions.explanation: Critical Geopolitics specifically shows that different registers of geopolitical discourse (academic, policy, popular) produce different and often contradictory representations of the same geographic and political realities. Policymakers use different geographic narratives than academics; media representations may differ from both. Analysing these differences is a core method of Critical Geopolitics.

questions.question: Regarding the symbolic dimension of contested spaces in geopolitics, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: How a contested territory is named and classified (e.g. 'occupied' vs 'integrated') carries significant political and legal implications
options.b: The symbolic meaning of locations such as Jerusalem, Crimea, or Taiwan shapes international legitimacy debates
options.c: Critical Geopolitics demonstrates that the symbolic and representational dimensions of territory are politically irrelevant — only physical control matters
options.d: Spatial representations in political discourse can normalise or delegitimise territorial claims

questions.explanation: Critical Geopolitics directly challenges this claim — it argues that symbolic and representational dimensions are politically significant, not irrelevant. How spaces are named, mapped, and described in political discourse shapes which claims are seen as legitimate. The debate over whether Crimea is 'Russian' or 'occupied Ukrainian' territory has concrete political and legal consequences even though physical control is not in dispute.

questions.question: Regarding maps as political documents, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Map projections systematically distort the relative size of geographic features, and different projections serve different purposes and political narratives
options.b: Choices about naming, border drawing, and inclusion/exclusion in maps reflect and reinforce power relations
options.c: Maps in geopolitical analysis are neutral technical documents that accurately capture objective geographic reality without political content
options.d: The political power of maps is demonstrated by historical examples like colonial border-drawing in Africa and the Middle East

questions.explanation: Maps are never politically neutral — this is a foundational insight of Critical Geopolitics. Projection choice (Mercator inflates northern territories), boundary lines (Berlin Conference's African borders), naming conventions, and what is shown or omitted all reflect political choices. Colonial cartography is a classic case: maps were instruments of territorial claim and legitimation, not neutral geographic records.

questions.question: Regarding EU defence spending and military capability before and after 2022, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Before 2022, most EU NATO members spent less than the 2% of GDP NATO defence spending target
options.b: Germany's 'Zeitenwende' (historic turning point) declaration in 2022 marked a major reversal of its post-WWII defence restraint
options.c: EU member states collectively exceeded US defence spending before Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine
options.d: The post-2022 period saw significant increases in European defence budgets and announcements of new military capability investments

questions.explanation: The US massively outspent European NATO members before 2022. The US accounted for roughly 70% of NATO's total defence spending. Most EU NATO members were below 2% of GDP. This asymmetry was a persistent source of US-Europe friction (burden-sharing debates). The EU collectively spent far less than the US, not more.

questions.question: Regarding the EU concept of 'open strategic autonomy,' which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Strategic autonomy refers to the EU's capacity to act independently in foreign and security policy when necessary
options.b: The 'open' qualifier signals that strategic autonomy is not isolationism — the EU remains committed to open trade and partnerships
options.c: Strategic autonomy means the EU seeks to decouple entirely from the US and pursue a fully independent geopolitical path
options.d: Strategic autonomy involves reducing critical dependencies in defence, technology, and energy while maintaining international cooperation

questions.explanation: 'Open strategic autonomy' is explicitly not about decoupling from the US or the West. The EU frames it as enhancing its capacity to act independently in specific circumstances while remaining embedded in its trans-Atlantic and multilateral partnerships. It is about selective de-risking of critical dependencies (Russian gas, Chinese critical inputs) rather than broad decoupling.

questions.question: Regarding 'Wandel durch Handel' (change through trade) as a foreign policy paradigm, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: 'Wandel durch Handel' is a German concept premised on the idea that economic integration would moderate authoritarian behaviour
options.b: The paradigm was applied to both Russia and China, expecting that trade and investment ties would incentivise political moderation
options.c: 'Wandel durch Handel' proved entirely correct in both the Russian and Chinese cases, with both states liberalising as predicted
options.d: Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine exposed the fundamental failure of 'Wandel durch Handel' as a policy framework

questions.explanation: The paradigm failed in both cases. Russia did not moderate — it invaded Ukraine despite deep economic integration with Europe. China did not liberalise — political repression intensified under Xi Jinping despite economic opening. These twin failures have fundamentally discredited the 'Wandel durch Handel' paradigm and driven the shift toward strategic autonomy and de-risking.

questions.question: Regarding the EU's post-2022 energy diversification from Russian gas, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: The EU rapidly accelerated LNG import capacity and diversified suppliers away from Russia after the 2022 invasion
options.b: By 2023, the US had become the largest supplier of LNG to the European Union
options.c: The EU replaced all Russian gas imports with alternative supplies within 90 days with no disruption to energy markets
options.d: Norway expanded its role as the EU's largest pipeline gas supplier following the reduction of Russian flows

questions.explanation: The energy diversification was a major and painful multi-year effort, not a 90-day seamless transition. It involved energy rationing, record-high gas prices, emergency LNG terminal construction, fuel switching, demand reduction, and significant economic cost. The disruption was severe — European gas prices reached record highs in 2022. Diversification was achieved but at substantial cost over years, not 90 days.

questions.question: Regarding the ideological divide between liberal democracies and autocracies in contemporary geopolitics, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Contemporary great power competition has an ideological dimension alongside the material power competition
options.b: China and Russia both challenge aspects of the liberal international order, though for different reasons and through different means
options.c: Ideological alignment between states is irrelevant to geopolitical alliances — states only align on the basis of material interests
options.d: Democracies show a tendency to cluster in security alliances (NATO, QUAD, AUKUS) while authoritarian states coordinate separately

questions.explanation: Ideology does influence geopolitical alignment, even if it is not the sole determinant. Democratic peace theory shows democracies rarely fight each other. The NATO alliance is composed entirely of democracies. China and Russia coordinate on defending state sovereignty against Western human rights norms — an ideological convergence. Values and interests interact rather than ideology being irrelevant.

questions.question: Regarding democratic peace theory, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Democratic peace theory holds that liberal democracies rarely go to war with each other
options.b: The empirical record shows very few if any wars between consolidated liberal democracies in the modern era
options.c: Democratic peace theory predicts that democracies never go to war with any country, including authoritarian states
options.d: Liberals argue that shared democratic institutions and norms create mutual restraint between democratic states

questions.explanation: Democratic peace theory is specifically a dyadic claim — democracies rarely fight each other — not a universal claim that democracies avoid war altogether. Democracies have frequently fought authoritarian states: Vietnam, Korea, Iraq, Afghanistan, Libya. The theory does not predict pacifism toward non-democracies, only relative peace in democratic-democratic dyads.

questions.question: Regarding China's promotion of its governance model, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: China actively promotes its development model as an alternative that separates economic growth from liberal democratic conditionality
options.b: China has used the UN Human Rights Council to defend state sovereignty against Western human rights interventionism
options.c: China presents its governance model as universally superior to all others and has imposed it as a condition for all economic partnerships
options.d: The attractiveness of the Beijing Consensus for some developing country governments lies in its 'no political strings' approach

questions.explanation: China does not impose its political system as a condition for economic partnerships — this is precisely the feature that distinguishes the Beijing Consensus from the Washington Consensus. China's pitch is explicitly non-interventionist: development finance without political or governance conditionality. Imposing its system would undermine this competitive advantage relative to Western development institutions.

questions.question: Regarding the Russia-China partnership and its ideological basis, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Russia and China have developed a 'comprehensive strategic partnership' since the 2010s
options.b: Both Russia and China share an interest in challenging US-led liberal international order and promoting 'multipolarity'
options.c: Russia and China form a formal ideological alliance based on shared communist ideology, equivalent to the Cold War Warsaw Pact
options.d: Despite cooperation, Russia and China have significant differences in interests and no formal military alliance

questions.explanation: Russia is no longer communist — it is a nationalist authoritarian state with an Orthodox-Christian identity project. China is communist in party structure but has embraced capitalism. Their partnership is pragmatic and anti-hegemonic (against US dominance) rather than ideologically unified. They have no formal military alliance — the relationship is explicitly termed a 'partnership,' not an alliance, and significant mutual suspicions persist.

questions.question: Regarding the construction of the Geopolitical Risk (GPR) index by Caldara and Iacoviello, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: The GPR index is constructed by counting newspaper articles containing geopolitical risk keywords as a share of total news coverage
options.b: The index tracks terms related to wars, terrorism, military tensions, nuclear weapons, and sanctions in major international newspapers
options.c: The GPR index is based on surveys of business executives asking them to rate current geopolitical risk levels
options.d: The GPR index allows historical comparison of geopolitical risk levels going back to 1900

questions.explanation: The GPR index uses automated text analysis of newspaper coverage, not surveys. This methodology allows historical backcasting (newspapers are archived) and provides a more systematic, less subjective measure than executive surveys. The survey-based approach is used in other risk indices (like Business Environment Risk Intelligence) but not in the Caldara-Iacoviello GPR index.

questions.question: Regarding the impact of geopolitical risk on financial markets and investment, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Elevated geopolitical risk typically increases uncertainty, raising the risk premium demanded by investors
options.b: Oil prices tend to spike during major geopolitical crises involving oil-producing regions
options.c: Academic research shows that geopolitical risk has no measurable impact on stock markets, investment flows, or commodity prices
options.d: The onset of major conflicts typically causes flight to safe-haven assets such as gold, US Treasuries, and the Swiss franc

questions.explanation: The academic literature, including Caldara and Iacoviello's own work, shows significant and measurable effects of geopolitical risk on economic variables. Elevated GPR is associated with lower investment, reduced economic activity, higher commodity price volatility, and equity market stress. The development of the GPR index was motivated precisely by the observed economic impact of geopolitical events.

questions.question: Regarding the predictability of geopolitical events using quantitative models, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Quantitative geopolitical risk models can identify elevated risk environments but generally cannot predict specific events with precision
options.b: Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine in February 2022 was assigned a high probability by most risk forecasting models months in advance
options.c: Black swan events — high-impact, low-probability geopolitical shocks — are by definition difficult to forecast quantitatively
options.d: The limits of geopolitical forecasting reflect the genuine unpredictability of human political decisions under uncertainty

questions.explanation: Most risk models significantly underestimated the probability of a full-scale Russian invasion of Ukraine until very shortly before it occurred. This is the classic example of geopolitical forecasting failure: even with satellite intelligence and diplomatic signals, the specific timing and scale were misjudged. This demonstrates that elevated risk environments (which models detected) do not reliably translate into accurate event prediction.

questions.question: Regarding the distinction between country risk and geopolitical risk, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Country risk encompasses domestic political instability, economic policy risk, and regulatory uncertainty within a single country
options.b: Geopolitical risk specifically concerns interstate conflict, military tensions, and international political dynamics
options.c: Country risk and geopolitical risk are identical concepts that always move together and require no separate analytical frameworks
options.d: A country can have elevated geopolitical risk (external military threat) while having stable domestic institutions and low country risk

questions.explanation: Country risk and geopolitical risk are related but analytically distinct. Taiwan has stable domestic institutions and governance (low country risk) but extremely high geopolitical risk (Chinese military threat). Conversely, many developing countries have high country risk (corruption, political instability) but relatively low geopolitical risk (no external military threat). Treating them as identical misses important dimensions of risk analysis.

questions.question: Regarding 'friend-shoring' as a corporate supply chain strategy, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: 'Friend-shoring' refers to redirecting supply chains toward suppliers in geopolitically aligned or trusted countries
options.b: Friend-shoring accepts higher costs in exchange for reduced geopolitical risk and supply chain vulnerability
options.c: Friend-shoring represents the same logic as efficiency-maximising globalisation, simply applied to a new set of suppliers
options.d: Friend-shoring is a response to the realisation that pure cost-efficiency optimisation ignores geopolitical and supply security considerations

questions.explanation: Friend-shoring represents a fundamentally different logic from efficiency-maximising globalisation. Traditional globalisation sought lowest-cost suppliers regardless of political context; friend-shoring explicitly sacrifices some efficiency for geopolitical security. The selection criterion changes from cost to political alignment and supply reliability. These are competing logics, not equivalent approaches applied to different supplier sets.

questions.question: Regarding secondary sanctions and their extraterritorial impact on business, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Secondary sanctions threaten non-US companies with exclusion from US markets if they transact with sanctioned entities
options.b: European companies faced compliance obligations under US Iran sanctions even though EU governments objected to their extraterritorial reach
options.c: Secondary sanctions apply only within US territory and have no effect on companies or transactions outside the United States
options.d: The extraterritorial reach of US financial power through dollar clearing gives US sanctions an effectiveness beyond US jurisdiction

questions.explanation: Secondary sanctions have explicitly extraterritorial reach — they affect non-US companies operating entirely outside the US. European banks, Asian trading companies, and multinationals have faced severe penalties for Iran, Russia, and Cuba-related transactions that violated US sanctions law. This extraterritoriality is controversial in international law but effective in practice because of dollar clearing dominance.

questions.question: Regarding geopolitical risks for Western firms operating in China, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Western firms in China face potential regulatory discrimination and market access restrictions if geopolitical tensions escalate
options.b: Firms with supply chains touching Xinjiang face reputational and compliance risks related to forced labour legislation
options.c: Operating in China presents only standard commercial risks equivalent to any other large emerging market with no geopolitical dimension
options.d: A rapid deterioration in US-China relations could expose Western firms to asset freezes, regulatory action, or market exclusion in China

questions.explanation: China presents distinctively elevated geopolitical risk beyond standard commercial risk. The risk of being caught between US-China sanctions regimes (operating in China while maintaining US compliance), supply chain exposure to Xinjiang forced labour legislation, potential Chinese retaliation against Western firms in any conflict scenario, and the specific risk of technology transfer requirements — all represent geopolitical risks with no analogue in standard emerging market country risk.

questions.question: Regarding the trade-off between supply chain efficiency and resilience, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Just-in-time supply chain management maximises efficiency but creates vulnerability to supply disruptions
options.b: Resilient supply chains require redundancy, inventory buffers, and multi-sourcing that add cost compared to lean systems
options.c: Companies can achieve both maximum supply chain efficiency and maximum resilience simultaneously without any trade-off
options.d: COVID-19 and geopolitical fragmentation exposed the vulnerabilities of efficiency-maximising global supply chains

questions.explanation: Efficiency and resilience are in fundamental tension — this is a core insight of supply chain risk management. Efficiency requires minimising inventory (just-in-time), single-sourcing for economies of scale, and locating production where costs are lowest. Resilience requires the opposite: buffer stocks, multiple suppliers, geographic diversification. These trade-offs cannot be eliminated; they must be managed through explicit prioritisation.

questions.question: Regarding critiques of geographic determinism in geopolitics, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Critics of geopolitical determinism argue that geography shapes but does not determine international outcomes
options.b: The 'against geopolitics' perspective holds that agency, norms, technology, and ideas also shape international politics
options.c: Critics of geopolitics argue that geography has absolutely no relevance to international relations and should be entirely discarded
options.d: Geographic factors can be overcome, circumvented, or transformed — as technology, economic integration, and institutions all demonstrate

questions.explanation: The critique targets determinism — the idea that geography mechanically determines outcomes — not geographic relevance entirely. Critics argue geography is one factor among many; they do not claim it has no relevance. The Netherlands' geography (coastal, rivers) clearly shapes its history and international role, but the Netherlands is not 'determined' to have any particular foreign policy by its geography.

questions.question: Regarding the agency of small and middle powers in geopolitical analysis, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Small states can exercise significant influence through control of strategic chokepoints, resources, or specialized capabilities
options.b: Middle powers can shape outcomes through coalition-building, multilateral diplomacy, and norm entrepreneurship
options.c: In a world of great power competition, small and middle powers have no meaningful agency and are entirely passive objects of great power politics
options.d: Singapore's geopolitical position controlling the Malacca Strait gives it leverage disproportionate to its size

questions.explanation: Small and middle powers exercise real agency. Singapore leverages its chokepoint position for economic and diplomatic influence. Qatar uses gas wealth and media (Al Jazeera) for outsized influence. Zelensky's Ukraine mobilised international support in ways that defied pure power-politics calculations. Geopolitical frameworks that focus exclusively on great powers miss how smaller actors shape outcomes.

questions.question: Regarding constructivism as a critique of geopolitical realism, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Constructivism argues that ideas, norms, and identity shape state behaviour beyond material factors like territory and military power
options.b: Constructivists point to how international norms (like the non-use of chemical weapons) shape state behaviour even without enforcement
options.c: Constructivism fully agrees with geopolitical realism that material power (territory, resources, military) is the sole determinant of international outcomes
options.d: The constructivist concept of 'security communities' explains why NATO members do not plan for war with each other despite differences in power

questions.explanation: Constructivism is a direct challenge to materialist realism. Where realism argues that material power determines behaviour, constructivism argues that shared identities, norms, and understandings structure international relations in ways that material calculations alone cannot explain. The EU is the constructivist paradigm case: economically and militarily capable states that have constructed a security community where war among members is inconceivable.

questions.question: Regarding the use of geopolitical discourse to legitimise imperialism, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Classical geopolitical thinkers like Haushofer provided intellectual frameworks that were used to justify Nazi expansionism
options.b: 19th-century geopolitical reasoning helped normalise colonial expansion as a natural expression of state geographic needs
options.c: All geopolitical thinkers throughout history have been critics of imperialism who used geographic analysis to expose injustice
options.d: Critical Geopolitics emerged partly as a response to the ways in which geographic discourse has been used to legitimate great power domination

questions.explanation: Classical geopolitical thinkers were largely imperialists themselves, not critics of imperialism. Mahan celebrated Anglo-Saxon naval power and American imperialism; Mackinder justified British global presence; Haushofer explicitly legitimised German expansion. The critique of geopolitics as a tool of imperial justification is one of the founding motivations of Critical Geopolitics as a sub-field.

questions.question: Regarding the 'crashing apps' metaphor used in the final lecture, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: The metaphor describes simultaneous failures across multiple domains of the post-Cold War order
options.b: The 'crashing apps' include: technology governance, international institutions, cultural norms, IR rules, geopolitical order, and political systems
options.c: The 'crashing apps' metaphor refers exclusively to failures in digital technology sectors and Silicon Valley business models
options.d: The simultaneous nature of these failures across domains makes the current period particularly turbulent and difficult to manage

questions.explanation: The 'crashing apps' metaphor is a broad systemic metaphor about multiple simultaneous failures across all dimensions of the global order — not a narrow reference to digital technology. The 'apps' crashing simultaneously include: technology (from common good to global threat), institutions (Washington Consensus in crisis), culture (cosmopolitan to localism), IR (return of zero-sum competition), geopolitics (hegemony to multipolar assertiveness), and political systems (rise of populism and autocracy).

questions.question: Regarding the four future scenarios presented in the final lecture, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: 'Slowbalization' is presented as suboptimal for everyone but potentially a stable equilibrium
options.b: 'Two Worlds Apart' (bloc division) is described as particularly damaging for developing countries caught between blocs
options.c: The 'Rise of Stakeholder Geopolitics' scenario represents a return to Cold War-style ideological bloc competition
options.d: Historical evidence is cited to show that 'Armageddon' scenarios are not theoretical — deglobalization and global conflict have occurred before

questions.explanation: 'Stakeholder Geopolitics' is the most optimistic scenario — it represents managed competition through negotiation and recognition of shared interests. It is the opposite of Cold War bloc competition. Cold War-style bloc division is captured by the 'Two Worlds Apart' scenario. Stakeholder Geopolitics implies actors accepting rules-based competition rather than zero-sum confrontation.

questions.question: Regarding globalization's distributional effects on equality, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: The second wave of globalization contributed to convergence between countries — developing nations grew faster than developed ones
options.b: Within countries, globalization's gains were concentrated and its losses distributed — particularly among lower-skilled workers in rich countries
options.c: Globalization produced greater equality both between countries AND within countries simultaneously in most cases
options.d: The political backlash against globalization — populism, protectionism — is linked to the 'within country' inequality it generated

questions.explanation: Globalization's effects on equality were divergent, not uniformly positive. Between countries: convergence (reduced inequality — developing nations caught up). Within countries: divergence (increased inequality — the top benefited most while lower-skilled workers in rich countries faced wage stagnation and deindustrialisation). This between/within distinction is crucial for understanding why globalization generated a populist backlash even as global poverty fell.
